sentences.text: Yes.
My father, I, I and my brother, who was 16 years old at the time-- I tried to, and I did, recruit him for a purpose, you  know?
But see, since my father was Jewish, you know, I gave him the papers, which he didn't have to wear stars or nothing.
And they went to the-- a camp-- what they call a pre-concentration camp-- to try to save people in Nyiregyhaza.
Shall I spell it for you?
N-Y-I-R-E-G-H-H-I-Z-A.
Hungarians don't have short names.
[LAUGHS]
And they were there in this camp.
And I went down in civilian clothes because I did not want to go down with German uniform at the time, you know?
Because I had my German papers, so I can go in civilian clothes the same way.
And I went into the camp, actually, and I talked to my father and my brother there.
You know?
And they told me.
He said, we have a suspicion they find out about us.
I told him, listen, what shall we do?
He says, we still try to help a couple people.
When can you come back?
This was about 150 miles from Budapest, you know?
So I told him, I tell you something.
I'll be back, probably tomorrow or a day after.
But I have to go back because I have an-- see, I could not go away from the headquarters, you know.
Because you know, you just-- I was in the German headquarters.
I just could not walk away, you know, because I would have lost whatever I worked for all along.
I came back, and I had to do-- go again to Mr.
Schweikert's place pick-- pick up people.
And I had to go to the island of Chapel Island when I met the partisans, you know, at a certain place.
And they are taking over the people there.
And the following day, I got the message that's my father and my brother was killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To find out about them, and they shot them right there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's the tragedy of life, you know.
You say, you-- you know, you do what you think is right to do, you know, and you can save your own family.
It's stuck with me for a long time, you know, but I just was out of my control what I could do, you know, really.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to live with it for a long time.
In fact, I'm still living with this, you know, really.
But I couldn't do nothing about it.
They did not want to leave because still had certain things to do there.

I could have taken with me right there and then, because I-- I not even used headquarters car for this, you know, because I-- I-- you know, if people would have find out, you know, they would get it all around the car, you know, a German military car, you know, and then somebody would recognize me.
As a civilian, I could go if without anybody really paying to-- any attention to me.
And I used the train back and forth, which was a short ride, train ride, you know.
So that's what I did.
And unfortunately, next day-- my aunt actually told me this.
When I told them I have to go to get my father, and my aunt told me, he said, it's-- you don't have to go.
He said, they are dead.
We just got information through the partisans.
So that's-- that was a downside of my whole assignment.
You know, when I could not save my father and my brother.
But I did whatever I could, you know, in order to justify my existence, so to speak, you know, really.
And I did it for simply one purpose, you know.
And it's very interesting.
You know, I heard a speech, which President Roosevelt made one time.
And the speech what he made was, we have to fight this war so no people have to fear to be persecuted because race, of religion, in this world.
And this was my guiding power, because I believed in the same thing, and I still believe in the same thing.
I can't remember anymore, but I distinctly remember, I know, that I heard the speech, and-- and-- or somebody quoted me the speech.
I can't remember this, but I do remember.
And it's-- it's-- he made the speech, I believe, at the beginning of the war, or somewhere, you know.
And I-- I know somebody-- somebody quoted for me, or-- And if I am not wrong, I believe I heard it on Radio Free Europe.
Or no, it wasn't free Europe.
What was that?
Voice of America or one of those things, you know?
I heard that, you know, because we used to go and bug.
We had a shortwave-- I mean, I still have it at home-- shortwave radio, which you can AM, FM, and shortwave.
You know, I'm still listen, many times, to different countries.
I'm still a buff, you know, of-- of politics and military, you know.
And I-- I-- I can swear I heard that, but I can't recall.
I wouldn't want to set a date or anything because I am not sure when I heard it, you know, or where I heard it, but I know I remember this.
You know, I heard it.
And to me it was a big influence, you know.

I-- the American movies with my childhood was a tremendous influence, you know, because I've seen there were a way-- I know it was a make-believe world, but when we're young, you know, a make-believe world is a real world for you.

And when you see all the Andy Hardy movies, you know, and you see all the cowboy movies, and all the good movies, you know, was made in-- in Hollywood, I had-- I had a feeling that I-- someday, I going to work there.
And I am going to be a cinematographer.
You know?
And I became one.
You know.
And unfortunately, I became a video-- videographer-- afterwards, which I hate, you know, because I love to work with films.
This was my first love, you know, really.
But I-- I felt-- actually, I almost left the country after I came here in 1951 for one, simple reason.
A-- no-- I went to a German club one time in-- in Pittsburgh, just to see what's going on, you know, and went to Hungarian club because you're new here.
You want to find out certain things.
And it's a German man was there who admitted proudly that he was in the German army, and he fought the Americans.
And I told him, how did you come to this country?
He says, hell.
He said, I told them-- I told them I was drafted, and everything, and they checked me out.
And I am right here.
And what bugged me the most is this.

You know, when this German man who came to the United States legally, who fought against the United States-- and I don't know how many Americans he killed-- the minute he stepped on the shore of the United States, he had more right than the black men who fought the war for the United States.
And it still bugs me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
It's-- maybe I am dead wrong saying all this, but I honestly felt this way, and I never ever went back to the German club after this.
This was my one and only visit.
I thought, I don't want to be associated with people like this.

And like I said, I still believe in-- in-- in the same way that-- I seen so much suffering because Hitler and his cohort felt people should be persecuted because according to them born in a wrong race, or a wrong religion, or a wrong nationality.
And I feel, you know, that I have to do my part to make good what bad was done before.
And I try to do it whenever I can.
And maybe-- maybe I'm an idealist, but sometime the world needs stupid idealists too.
Yes.
I have no idea how they picked them up.
Apparently, you know, someone-- they did something.
You know, they weren't schooled enough to work in the intelligence.
You know.
And believe me, it did-- there were so many Hungarian Nazis.
You know.
Then did not take too much, you know, someone who maybe not recognized them, but had ill will toward them.
So how did that happen?
I never know, really.
I wanted to find out.
In fact, that's the reason I went back to Nyirbator after the war.
I was talking to a friend of mine there-- to if he can give me some information, you know?
And unfortunately, he passed away last year, you know.
And his name was [?
Joseph ?] [?
Fisher. ?]
And they just could not find out what happened.
But he told me this, that the Nazis became Nazis, Hungarian Nazis, not really because they-- they hated the Jews, but this gave them a chance to rob their belongings.
See, because when their people were deported, the Hungarian trash, so to speak, went to their homes and lived in their homes, took their belongings.
And that's, naturally, they wanted to make sure they never come back because then they would have to give back.
That's happened in 1947 in Poland.
And the name, I believe, was [INAUDIBLE].

When a half a dozen Jewish people came back-- you know, they went to Germany first-- and they came back and just asked their people-- their-- their local government to give back their home, what they owned before the war.
And the-- and the local people did a pogrom and killed all the people.
This was two years after the war.
So you know, the-- the-- the Hungarians, like many other people, there were a percentage of people who would do for greed everything.
And they would kill the people because they can profit by.
It-- it-- it wasn't what you may call a real, pure anti-Semitism.
They would have killed their own brother if they could have profit by many.
But legally, they could kill the Jews at the time, legally.
And can profit by taking their belongings.
They're taking their homes and whatever they had.
You know.
That's it.

I know because this friend of mine told me that-- that after the Jews was deported from the [INAUDIBLE], he said, you'd see every Jewish home, dozens of people digging everywhere, see if the Jews hid something, you know, or-- or-- or put something, in case if they come back, you know, and hide away.
Yes.
Yes.
It was in-- in a-- what they call-- this wasn't the regular railroad station.
It was like-- like a freight station, you know.
They're called a [INAUDIBLE].
It mean the west [GERMAN], west station.
OK.
They took the people.
First of all, what they did, they took the people in a big room.
They-- they told them this-- take all your valuables with you.
Now, when they came to the train station, then they told them, you just go up there, and we put all your belongings after you.
They never did nothing.
As soon as the train was full, they shut the door.
And they ravaged through what the people belonging they were.
You know, everything.
You know, they took everything away.
I mean, whatever was useful.
You know.
In fact, you know, in the-- let me recall the date.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1944-- 1944, the Hungarians-- it wasn't the Germans-- Hungarian Nazis took approximately 2,700 Jewish people to the Danube River, to the wharf.
And I went through the bridge, which was-- wasn't far away from there, and I could not stop.
I just had a glance of it.
And I find out next day that they executed 1,700 people, and they made a hole on the ice because the Danube was frozen.
And the people-- some of them wasn't even shot fatally.
Just throw them in the river, and they threw the children live into the river. and they have to be all naked.
They took all their clothes off.
And they took all the clothes and donated it for the Sudetendeutsche who had to flee the Russians.
I find this out next day when I went back to the SS headquarters, what they did.
And they even, the SS had-- you know, I-- I had one good friend in the SS.
This was a little-- he was a vermittlung, what they call vermittlung, a switchboard operator.
OK?
He was-- he was a kid from Austria, from Vienna, and we became close because part of my family was from Vienna.
And he told me, he said, I hate this war and hate what's going on.
And he says, I just-- I just want them to get it over with, and I can go back home to Vienna.
And he's the one who fed me a lot of information because we became kind of a confidant.
He was became-- and so I find out a lot of things from him.
You know, when the trains coming.
You know, because he is a-- as a switchboard operator know-- because, you know, to send all his guards out, you know, or whatever he had to do to the trains.
And he had all this information.
You know?
What I find out when Eichmann came to town-- Eichmann was actually-- you know, was-- came only-- he made arrangement with the Jewish leadership.
That's all, you know.
And he was in a [INAUDIBLE] palace.
You know, in-- up-- up in the-- near the king's palace.
You know.
That's all he was.
Except [INAUDIBLE] and he run around in town.
They checked all of his SS, different SS outposts.
But I was able to find out a lot of information, you know, really-- what was like troop movements.
When they called back troops, you know, from one front to another.
Actually, what Hitler wanted, he wanted to make Hungary as a fighting point, so they let ruin Hungary instead of Austria or Germany.
Whatever he want, you know.
That's what he want-- wanted to do.
So he pulled all the forces, you know, one time, and put them in the Hungarian border, but the Russian's forces were so tremendous really.
And the German soldiers at that time, they were lack of everything from-- from lack of a food, to lack of spirit, and lack of ammunition and everything.
You know, they weren't a fighting force anymore, you know, really.
They were nothing, just a scratch force, and-- which was fortunate, you know, at the time.
You know, that's they way they were.
But I honestly feel this way, that the majority of the killings, they weren't done by the Germans itself.
They may ordered by the Germans.
I don't question this.
And I don't question just they may supervise it, but the actual killing was done by the Polish SS, by the Croatian SS, by the Baltic SS, and the Hungarian arrowheads.
They were actually the lord's high executioners.
You know.
And then, after the war, quite a few were put in trial in Hungary, and they were executed.
You know why.
But for-- you know, too many escaped, you know, really, who actually was responsible for the killing.
And they were-- it wasn't hesitant, you know.
If somebody was any doubt, just shoot him.
First of all, they'd set up a hotel, the Hotel Metropol in Budapest, with Jewish women, you know, to serve as prostitutes for the German SS, and also for the-- for the Arrow Cross people.
Now, here is the irony of this.
They don't want the races to mix, but the Jewish woman was good enough to fulfill their needs.
You know?
And-- and just so many-- so many ironies of this whole situation was-- you know?
I just like when Mussolini had to execute his own son-in-law, Count Ciano, because he tried to make arrangement for a separate peace movement.
When Hitler find out, he ordered Mussolini to execute, you know, his own son-in-law-- as married to Edda Mussolini.
You know.
Go ahead.
Can we go back to the Hotel Metropol?
Yes.
I'll tell you something, how I find out.
Because after the war, a very good friend of mine owned the hotel, and she told me exactly what was going on.
I never, personally, was there in the place, you know?
Or I ever witnessed anything, but she who owned the hotel after the war told me exactly.
I tell you whose hangout was this place, also-- Zsa Zsa Gabor's father.
He was a-- he was a-- what do you call-- a guest, you know, steady guest in this-- [? best ?] place.
And at a little bar called the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], which means some kind of a flower, Hungarian flower.
He used to go there always with real young girls.
I seen him quite a few times there, you know, his father.
And-- and Magda, you know, the oldest one, I mean she was serving the German army with passion.
I mean, I just say this.
That's where I'm talking about them, really.
Yes.
Oh, I was in a ghetto area quite a few times.
You know, I went through my corridor.
I never walked through.
You know, I never walked through.
I just always drove through the ghetto area.
Since the ghetto area was only maybe half a block away where our safe place was.
In fact, part of a safe-- the street-- part of a street was the ghetto area, you know.
Not where we were, but maybe four houses, you know, of the street.
There was a fence was set up for the ghetto there.
A fence area was set up, in [INAUDIBLE].
And I went to-- there were certain places where you can enter with the car, and me, I had no problem, you know, with the German military car to go through the fence, you know?
And the people-- how can I explain to you?
You know, they did not live there.
They existed there only, you know, because [? table-- ?] very little food was available.
You know?

And some of the waterworks wasn't working, you know, because they were bombed, you know, and the bombing was done by English and American fliers, you know, during-- the English in daytime and Americans at night.
Or-- or I believe-- I can't remember.
Yes.
And the-- the people were just-- all these 300,000 people were put together to an area which normally would live maybe 30,000.
And there were just-- see, this was in October.
The weather wasn't that bad at the time, and by the weather that's so bad, most of them were deported.
You know?
But later on, they were deported, most of them.
But at the time, you know, in the early days when I was there, you know, one deported.
And I did not know who executed the people, but I went through one time-- what was the name of the street?
[INAUDIBLE]-- no.
[INAUDIBLE], I believe that was the name.
When I seen, I would say, maybe four block-- blocks long people piled up like cardboard dead people.
Now, I don't know if they were all Jews.
I could not tell this, you know, really, because I did not do stop there and see it.
Only I stopped for one time there, and I seen, and then somebody try-- people were looking for relatives, you know?
And that was in-- in December.
And-- in-- in 1944-- and some of the bodies were frozen so much, you know, then when people tried to pick it up, just broke off.
You know.
And-- and I seen this one, but who did the killing?
I could not tell, you know, really.
I know the people were there, you know, like piled up like cardboard, you know.
That's the way it were there.
But in-- in-- and in the ghetto itself, there wasn't too much of killing because they just robbed the people there-- you know, all their belongings.
You know, there was nothing unusual, you know, that people, you know, like the-- the-- the SS did not go into the ghettos.
There was a Hungarian Arrow Cross.
You know.
And there was nothing unusual to see somebody with a watch-- stop them, take the watch off.
You know, or take a-- seen a good-looking woman, just took with them.
Where they took them, I don't know, but took with them, you know.

And I seen this happen, but I cannot be overly, you know, suspicious to go there and look everything, or question anything like it, you know, because I didn't want to blow my cover, so to speak-- you know, or put not just me, but the whole organization of danger.
Because as far as I know, I was the only inside man they had.
I was the only one who can give them the information, as far as I know.
And-- and the only person who was my contact man was Mr.
Schweikert.
If he had more, I don't know about it.
And-- and the Swedish diplomat, I didn't know until the day he was taken with these Russian guards.
He was Raoul Wallenberg.

I know he was a Swedish diplomat because-- you know, the less I learned by being taught by very good-- by some people who were in intelligence and the resistance-- you don't ask questions, and you don't remember names.
Because if you get captured, everybody has a certain point of resistance when you just break.
If you don't know, you can say nothing.

But if you know something, you know, to come to a breaking point when you want to spill everything hoping to save you-- and if you don't know, you don't have to worry about anything because you can-- they kill you anyhow.
That was their-- their favorite thing.

The favorite thing was their-- their-- for the SS and the-- and-- and the Nazis, when they arrested maybe three people, and they took them up, for instance, in the SS headquarters, and demand one man to let three men sit together and ask them questions.
And if one man aggravated them, wouldn't talk, they took him to another room, and shot off the pistol.
It not necessarily-- I don't think so they killed him, but the other two people thought the other man was shot.
You understand?
And they are right away started to talking.
And then, later on, to find the man back in the prison with them.
He said, we thought you were shot.
This was a favorite trick they used.
We're going to change the reel.
